title: Two 18-20k-line God-file monoliths (routes.ts, storage.ts) concentrate the whole server surface
summary: server/routes.ts is 20,566 lines and server/storage.ts is 18,036 lines — 38.6k lines (37% of the 103.5k-line server) live in two files. All HTTP routing/business logic and all persistence sit in single modules with no enforced boundary.
evidence: `wc -l server/routes.ts` = 20566; `wc -l server/storage.ts` = 18036; next-largest server file is seed.ts at 4257.
impact: Change-safety + review blast radius: any route/persistence edit risks unrelated breakage; merge conflicts are near-certain under concurrent work (this fleet's own YCM builds collided repeatedly); tenant-isolation review (top risk #1) must be done per-call-site across ~515 routes in one file with no module seam to reason about. Slows every future change and hides coupling.
validation: Line counts confirmed in a clean origin/main worktree.
remediation_direction: Incrementally carve domain routers out of routes.ts (payments, amenities, reconciliation, portal, admin) behind an Express Router per domain; split storage.ts into per-aggregate repositories. Do it move-only (no behavior change) with the existing tests as the safety net. No big-bang rewrite.
regression_risk: medium — mechanical extraction can drop a route registration or middleware order; mitigated by move-only commits + route-inventory diff.
tests_required: route-inventory snapshot (count + paths) before/after each extraction
limitations: Did not enumerate circular-dependency cycles statically (no madge run — read-only, no install); coupling asserted from size + single-file concentration, not a dependency graph.

title: Postgres pool has no max configured → pg default of 10 connections caps throughput under the automation sweep
summary: The single shared pg.Pool sets connectionTimeoutMillis + idleTimeoutMillis but NOT `max`, so node-postgres defaults to max=10 connections for the entire process — shared by all HTTP request handlers AND the monolithic 5-minute automation sweep that fans out many sub-sweeps.
evidence: server/db.ts:9 `export const pool = new pg.Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL, connectionTimeoutMillis: 10000, idleTimeoutMillis: 30000 });` — no `max`.
impact: With fly.toml hard_limit=250 concurrent requests on one machine plus a heavy periodic sweep, 10 pooled connections is a throughput ceiling: excess queries queue up to the 10s connectionTimeout then throw. Under load or during a long sweep this surfaces as intermittent request timeouts/5xx that look random. Neon also imposes its own connection ceiling, so the number must be chosen against Neon's limit, not maximized blindly.
validation: Pool config read directly; default-max behavior is node-postgres documented default (10).
remediation_direction: Set an explicit `max` sized to Neon's connection limit for the plan (and reserve headroom for the sweep), e.g. max=15-20 with a Neon pooler (PgBouncer/`-pooler` host) in front; consider a separate small pool for the background sweep so it can't starve request handlers.
regression_risk: low — additive config; wrong (too-high) value could hit Neon's ceiling, so verify against the plan limit.
tests_required: load test at expected concurrency observing pool wait time
confirm Neon max_connections for the current plan
limitations: Did not measure live pool saturation (no prod access); impact is inferred from config + concurrency limit, not observed.

title: Six in-process setInterval schedulers + the automation sweep are guarded only by in-memory state → duplicate money actions on any multi-machine scale-out
summary: All scheduled work runs as in-process setInterval timers, and the monolithic automation sweep (autopay, delinquency notices, assessment dispatch, bank-feed sync, etc.) is deduped only by an in-memory `globalAutomationState` flag. There is no cross-process lock. Safe today only because fly.toml pins min_machines_running=1; the moment a second machine runs (scale-out, or an overlap during a rolling deploy), every timer fires on every machine.
evidence: server/index.ts ~340 `const timer = setInterval(() => { runAutomationSweep()... })`; dedupe is `globalAutomationState.__automationJobState` (in-mem, per-process). fly.toml:44 `auto_stop_machines = "stop"`, :45 `auto_start_machines = true`, :46 `min_machines_running = 1`; a fly.toml comment itself notes "For future multi-machine scale-out...". Five more setInterval schedulers at the listed locations.
impact: On >1 machine the autopay sweep double-charges owners, delinquency notices/emails double-send, and assessment dispatch duplicates — real-money and owner-facing blast radius. Even single-machine, a rolling deploy briefly runs old+new instances, so an overlapping sweep window can double-fire. This is the structural root of the 'scheduled jobs' fragility.
validation: Timers + in-mem guard read directly; scale config read from fly.toml.
remediation_direction: Move the sweep dedupe to a cross-process lock (Postgres advisory lock `pg_try_advisory_lock` around each sweep, keyed per sweep type) BEFORE any scale-out; longer term move scheduling to a durable queue/leader-election. Gate the fix so it is a no-op on single-machine.
regression_risk: medium — an advisory lock that isn't released on crash could stall a sweep; use a session-scoped lock that auto-releases on disconnect.
tests_required: two-instance test proving only one sweep runs
limitations: Did not confirm whether rolling deploys actually overlap sweep windows in prod; double-fire on scale-out is deductive from in-mem-only dedupe.

title: Google Maps API key committed in fly.toml — cost/quota-theft exposure if the key is not usage-restricted
summary: A live Google Maps API key is committed in fly.toml (`VITE_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY = "AIza..."`). Because it's a VITE_ variable it ships to the browser bundle anyway (not a secret leak), but a committed, client-exposed Maps key that lacks HTTP-referrer + API restrictions is directly abusable → third-party quota consumption billed to the account.
impact: If unrestricted, any actor can pull the key from the JS bundle and drive Maps API calls on William's billing — an unbounded cost meter. Also in git history, so rotation requires a real key roll, not just a value change.
validation: Key presence confirmed in fly.toml; restriction status NOT verified (requires Google Cloud console access, out of read-only scope).
remediation_direction: In Google Cloud console: restrict the key to (1) the production HTTP referrer(s) and (2) only the Maps JS/Places APIs actually used; then rotate the committed key (it is in git history) and inject the new one as a Fly secret consumed at build. Add a quota cap alert.
regression_risk: low — referrer restriction can break local/preview origins if too narrow; include dev origins.
tests_required: confirm map still renders from the prod origin after restriction
confirm a non-allowed origin is rejected
limitations: Cannot see the key's current restriction config from the repo; severity is conditional on it being unrestricted.

title: Low explicit-index-to-table ratio (47 indexes / 183 tables) suggests missing composite indexes on hot multi-tenant query paths
summary: schema.ts declares 47 explicit index() definitions across 183 tables (~0.26 per table). For a multi-tenant financial app whose hot queries filter by association_id + status + date, that ratio hints many tables rely on the PK only, leaving common filtered/sorted reads to sequential scans as data grows.
impact: As per-association row counts grow (ledger entries, transactions, notices), unindexed association_id+status filters degrade to seq scans → slow dashboards and slow sweeps (compounding PERF-B-002's pool pressure). Financial reads are the hottest path.
validation: Ratio computed; SPECIFIC missing indexes NOT confirmed — no EXPLAIN/query-plan access in read-only scope.
remediation_direction: Capture pg_stat_statements from prod, EXPLAIN the top filtered queries (association_id/status/date), and add composite indexes where a seq scan on a large table appears. Add indexes migration-by-migration, verify each with EXPLAIN.
regression_risk: low — additive indexes; watch write amplification on very hot insert tables.
tests_required: EXPLAIN before/after on the top-5 filtered queries
limitations: This is a heuristic signal (ratio), not a proven missing-index list; needs runtime query-plan verification to promote to validated.

title: Database seeding runs on every process boot (await seedDatabase) — boot latency + correctness risk if the seed is not fully idempotent
summary: server/index.ts awaits seedDatabase() during startup. seed.ts is 4,257 lines. With Fly auto_stop/auto_start, a cold start pays the full seed cost before the server accepts traffic, and any non-idempotent seed step could mutate prod data on each restart.
evidence: server/index.ts:564 `await seedDatabase();` inside the boot path; seed.ts = 4257 lines.
impact: Cold-start latency after auto_stop (first request after idle waits for seed); if any seed write is not guarded by an existence check, restarts could re-insert/reset data. On a live customer's books that is a data-integrity risk, not just a perf one.
validation: Boot-path call confirmed; seed idempotency NOT verified line-by-line (4257 lines, out of proportional read-only budget).
remediation_direction: Gate seedDatabase() behind an env flag (seed only in dev/CI, never on prod boot), or assert every seed write is upsert/guard-checked; move seeding to an explicit one-shot migration step rather than the request-serving boot path.
regression_risk: low — flag-gating is additive; ensure prod still has its required reference rows via migration.
tests_required: boot with seed disabled still serves
re-running seed twice is a no-op (row counts stable)
limitations: Did not read all 4257 seed lines; idempotency assumed-uncertain, hence low+needs-verification.

title: POSITIVE / hypothesis-refuted: money-moving Stripe raw-fetch calls DO carry Idempotency-Key (no double-charge gap found)
summary: The mapper's risk #2 hypothesised idempotency gaps in the raw-fetch (no-SDK) Stripe calls. Verified the money-moving POSTs and every one applies an Idempotency-Key: checkout/sessions (payment-service:198 + routes:6222 passthrough), off-session payment_intents (payment-service:653), and the amenity gateway (:90, keyed by caller). The double-charge-on-retry risk is largely refuted for the money paths inspected.
evidence: payment-service.ts:653 `intentHeaders["Idempotency-Key"] = offSessionChargeKey(params.transactionId)`; :198 checkout key; amenity-stripe-gateway.ts:90 `if (idempotencyKey) h["Idempotency-Key"] = idempotencyKey`; routes.ts:6222 `stripeHeaders["Idempotency-Key"] = paymentLinkCheckoutKey(...)`.
impact: Reduces the payment-integrity risk surface: retries of these charges return the original intent rather than re-charging.
validation: Read each POST's header construction directly.
remediation_direction: Keep the safety centralized: the amenity gateway only sets the key IF the caller passes one (`if (idempotencyKey)`) — make the key non-optional / assert-present at the fetch wrapper so a future call site can't silently omit it. That's the only residual: idempotency is per-call-site convention, not enforced by a shared client.
limitations: Did not exhaustively audit every one of the 8 stripe fetch sites' headers — verified the money-moving ones; read-only usage-reconcile/search calls are non-mutating.

title: POSITIVE: bank-feed reconciliation matcher is well-designed (integer cents, confidence-scored, conflict-safe) — risk #8 largely holds up
summary: The reconciliation auto-matcher (mapper risk #8) does money math in integer cents (amountCents, Math.round(dollars*100)), scores matches 0..1 across amount/date/payor signals, only auto-applies above AUTO_MATCH_THRESHOLD=0.85, surfaces borderline candidates to the admin UI, and has an explicit conflict rule when multiple ledger entries exceed the threshold. Correctness posture is sound.
evidence: auto-matcher.ts header comment (:19-47) documents confidence tiers + conflict rule; :527 `const creditAbsCents = Math.abs(credit.amountCents)`; :546 `Math.round(Math.abs(entry.amount) * 100)`.
impact: Auto-application is bounded (threshold + conflict rule + human review of borderline), so silent mis-postings are unlikely.
validation: Read the matcher's thresholds + cents handling directly.
remediation_direction: Minor: the ledger side stores amount as a float dollars field (`entry.amount`) converted to cents via Math.round while the bank side is native integer amountCents — two money representations in one domain. Standardize on integer cents end-to-end to remove the conversion seam.
tests_required: property test: round-trip dollars↔cents never drifts a penny across the match range
limitations: Reviewed matcher thresholds + a sample of the money-math sites, not all 1272 lines of auto-matcher.ts nor the posting side end-to-end.

title: Money-moving multi-row writes are NOT transactional — server uses db.transaction exactly once (a non-money RAG path), so a mid-operation failure leaves partial financial state
summary: Across the entire server, drizzle db.transaction( is called exactly ONCE and only in the non-money RAG ingester (server/services/rag/ingester.ts); there is no raw BEGIN/COMMIT or withTransaction wrapper anywhere else. So every multi-write money operation runs as a sequence of independent awaits with no atomic boundary. Clearest instance: importBankStatementRecord (storage.ts:11148) loops over parsed bank transactions and creates each owner-ledger money entry via createOwnerLedgerEntry (storage.ts:11276) one await at a time; a failure/crash/rolling-restart partway through the loop leaves a partially-imported statement (some ledger rows written, some not, no rollback). Route-level ledger posts (routes.ts:6668/7004/15616) are likewise single-write, un-transactioned.
evidence: storage.ts:11276 'const created = await this.createOwnerLedgerEntry({...})' inside the per-row loop of 'private async importBankStatementRecord(' at storage.ts:11148, with no enclosing db.transaction; repo-wide grep of server/ for the drizzle transaction call returns exactly 1 hit, in server/services/rag/ingester.ts (non-money).
impact: Partial financial writes on any mid-operation failure (error, OOM, or the SCALE-B-003 single-machine restart) leave inconsistent owner ledgers / assessment balances requiring manual reconciliation. Blast radius = owner-ledger correctness (the delinquency + payment source-of-truth). Mitigated for RE-RUNS by the per-row referenceId idempotency check (storage.ts:11259-11273 skips already-imported rows), but NOT for the partial-state window between the failure and the re-run.
validation: Source-confirmed in a clean origin/main worktree: the single db.transaction site, the un-wrapped import loop, and the 3 route callers all read directly. The runtime frequency of partial-failure is the hypothesis half — needs fault-injection to quantify.
remediation_direction: Wrap each multi-row money operation (bank-statement import loop; any create-charge + post-ledger + update-balance sequence) in a single drizzle db.transaction so it commits atomically or rolls back; keep the per-row idempotency for re-run safety. A shared postToLedger(tx) helper would make new call sites inherit atomicity.
regression_risk: low-medium — wrapping sequential writes in a transaction changes failure semantics to all-or-nothing (the intent); must confirm no long-running import loop holds a tx open past Neon statement/idle timeout (batch large imports).
tests_required: fault-injection: fail mid bank-statement import, assert zero partial ledger rows (rollback)
re-run idempotency still holds after a rolled-back import
limitations: Read-only, no runtime/prod DB — partial-failure blast radius quantified from source structure, not observed in production.
